title: Kerala Parotta

overview.description: A soft, flaky South Indian flatbread made from an elastic plain-flour dough that is stretched, layered, coiled and cooked on a hot tawa.

ingredients: Extra neutral oil, for resting and layering
method: Mix the flour, salt and sugar in a large bowl.
Add the oil.
Gradually add the water, mixing until a soft, slightly sticky dough forms.
Knead for 10–15 minutes until the dough becomes smooth and elastic.
Cover and rest for at least 1 hour.
Divide the dough into 8 equal portions.
Coat each portion generously with oil and leave to rest for a further 30 minutes.
Take one piece of dough and place it on a lightly oiled work surface.
Stretch and gently pull the dough outwards until it becomes very thin. It does not need to be perfectly transparent, but should be much thinner than a normal flatbread.
Brush the surface lightly with oil.
Fold or pleat the dough into a long strip.
Coil the strip into a spiral and tuck the end underneath.
Flatten the coil gently with your hand.
Repeat with the remaining dough and rest the coils for 10–15 minutes.
Roll or press each coil into a thick, roughly circular flatbread.
Heat a dry tawa or heavy frying pan over medium heat.
Cook the parotta until the underside develops golden-brown spots.
Turn and cook the other side.
Brush lightly with oil if desired.
Once cooked, place the parotta between your hands and gently clap or scrunch it to separate the layers.

learning.did-you-know: Kerala parotta is made using a technique of stretching, layering and coiling dough that creates its characteristic flaky, almost pastry-like texture.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How gluten development, hydration, oil and repeated layering can transform a simple flour dough into a soft, flaky flatbread.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A distinctive South Indian flatbread that demonstrates how a highly hydrated, elastic dough can be transformed into delicate layers through stretching and coiling.
chapman-tips: The dough should be soft and elastic rather than stiff.
The long resting time is important because it allows the gluten to relax, making the dough much easier to stretch.
Use plenty of oil during resting; this helps prevent the dough from sticking and makes stretching easier.
Don't be afraid to stretch the dough very thinly.
If the dough keeps springing back, it needs more resting time.
The final parotta should be thicker than the stretched dough but still contain many thin layers.
Gently clapping or scrunching the cooked parotta helps separate the layers.
notes.batch-cooking: Cooked parotta freezes well. Cool completely, stack with baking paper between them if desired and freeze in an airtight bag or container. Reheat directly from frozen in a dry frying pan.
notes.leftovers: Use leftover parotta with eggs, cut into pieces for dipping or chop it into a kothu parotta-style dish.
